title: Reproducibility
concept: Reproducibility means that a result can be recreated with the same code, settings, data, and environment, so progress depends on stable evidence rather than one lucky run on one specific machine.

exercises.prompt: Why is reproducibility valuable in machine learning?

options.text: Because a result is much more trustworthy when you can recreate it.
Because training should always be random and mysterious.
Because seeds and configs have no effect on outcomes.

exercises.explain: Exactly. If a result can be recreated, it becomes stronger evidence.

cards.title: One amazing run is not the same as a reliable result
cards.body: A team reaches 94% validation accuracy, celebrates, and then reruns the same project next week and gets 90%. What changed? If the answer is “we are not sure,” the team has a reproducibility problem.

cards.title: What reproducibility means in practice
cards.body: Reproducibility means someone can run the same experiment again and get essentially the same conclusion because the seed, configuration, code, data split, and software environment were kept consistent and recorded clearly.

cards.title: A recipe that works in any kitchen
cards.body: If a cake recipe only succeeds once and nobody knows the oven temperature, ingredient amounts, or baking time, it is not a dependable recipe. Reproducibility in deep learning is the same idea: write the recipe well enough that another run can follow it.

cards.title: More than just setting a seed
cards.body: Suppose two engineers both use seed 42, but one has a different library version and a changed preprocessing script. Their results may still drift. Reproducibility is broader than one random seed; it includes the full setup around the run.

cards.title: A seed helps, but it is not magic
cards.body: A common myth is that setting the seed solves reproducibility by itself. Seeds matter, but they are only one piece. If configs, code, data versions, or environments change, the result can still move.

cards.title: Lucky run vs. repeatable process
cards.body: Reliable teams try to make results survive a rerun, not just one exciting screenshot.

left.text:
Seed may be missing.
Configs live in memory or chat logs.
Another engineer struggles to repeat the run.

right.text:
Seeds and configs are recorded.
Data and code versions are clear.
A rerun gives the same story again.

options.text: Saving the config, seed, and dataset version with the result.
Changing preprocessing without writing it down.
Depending on memory instead of saved settings.

exercises.explain: Right. Reproducibility improves when the full setup is recorded, not just the final metric.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Using the same random seed can help reproducibility, but it does not replace environment and config tracking.
exercises.explain: True. Reproducibility is a whole-system habit, not a one-number trick.

exercises.sentence: If another engineer cannot recreate your result, the experiment is not fully ___.
exercises.explain: Reproducible is correct. The point is that the result should survive being repeated.

exercises.prompt: Which team is taking reproducibility seriously?

options.text: They save seeds, configs, library versions, and the exact train/validation split.
They rely on one lucky score and never rerun it.
They change two scripts and assume nothing important changed.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Reproducibility comes from controlling and recording the full setup.

exercises.prompt: A teammate reruns your project with the same model idea but gets a very different outcome. You discover the seed, preprocessing config, and library versions were never saved. What principle does this highlight?

options.text: Reproducibility needs seeds, configs, and environment consistency, not just a remembered idea.
Different outcomes prove reproducibility is impossible.
The best fix is to stop documenting experiments.
Only final accuracy matters, not how stable it is.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Stable results depend on recording the full recipe, not only the model name.
